Subject.title: Subject
Subject.description: Represents a single academic subject or course for which a student tracks attendance. This entity stores all relevant attendance statistics and metadata.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Subject entity.

name.description: The name of the subject or course, e.g., 'Introduction to Computer Science'.

attendanceRequirementPercentage.description: The minimum attendance percentage required for this subject, a value between 1 and 100.

teacherName.description: The name of the teacher or instructor for this subject. This field is optional.

totalClasses.description: The total number of classes conducted or recorded for this subject, including both present and absent counts.

presentClasses.description: The count of classes the student has marked as 'present' for this subject.

absentClasses.description: The count of classes the student has marked as 'absent' for this subject.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the subject was first created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp of the last time any attendance was marked or subject details were updated.

definition.description: Stores individual academic subjects and their associated attendance data for a specific user. Ownership is intrinsically tied to the '{userId}' in the path, ensuring direct and secure access control by the authenticated user.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this subject data (corresponding to 'request.auth.uid').

params.name: subjectId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific academic subject.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for the ClassTrack application prioritizes secure, scalable, and debuggable authorization for user-owned attendance data. Following the core design principles, especially Authorization Independence and Structural Segregation, all subject data for a specific user will be stored under a hierarchical path: `/users/{userId}/subjects/{subjectId}`. This structure ensures that each user's data is isolated and directly addressable.

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):** This structure achieves Authorization Independence by embedding the owner's identifier (`{userId}`) directly into the document path. For any document at `/users/{userId}/subjects/{subjectId}`, the `userId` wildcard explicitly represents the owner. This means that Firestore Security Rules can authorize access simply by comparing `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` in the path (e.g., `allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId;`). This eliminates the need for `get()` calls to a parent user document or to explicitly denormalize an `ownerId` field within the `Subject` document for authorization purposes, making rules atomic, simple to write, and highly performant.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** This design inherently supports Querying All Possibilities (QAPs). When a user queries for their subjects, they will issue a query like `db.collection('users').doc(request.auth.uid).collection('subjects').get()`. The security rules will directly evaluate `request.auth.uid == userId` for all documents in the `/users/{userId}/subjects` subcollection. Only documents where this condition is true (i.e., only the current user's subjects) will be returned. This ensures that the client application never receives unauthorized data, and the security rules do not act as data filters on a broader, less secure dataset. The structure inherently segregates data by user, providing a homogeneous security posture for all documents within a given user's `subjects` subcollection.
